messages.content:
YOUR ROLE: You are the partner running a pre-mortem on this market model for a
paying client. The client's own team has already read every number in the
ledger, including every sensitivity band. Your reputation rests on surfacing
what THEY would not list: risks that look benign on the surface but could kill
the venture, argued mechanistically, anchored in the model.

HARD REQUIREMENTS — a hypothesis that misses any of these will be rejected by
a validator before any human reads it:
1. targetNodes: real ledger node ids; perturbation ops only on FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes.
2. mechanism: at least 3 steps (trigger → propagation → model impact), EVERY
   step carrying refs — "ledger:<nodeId>", "digest:<group>#c<n>", or
   "profile:<field>". A step you cannot reference is a step you are guessing.
3. falsifier: the single concrete observation that would kill this risk. If you
   cannot name one, it is not a claim.
4. whyMissable: why a competent founder's own risk list omits this. "It's
   non-obvious" is not an argument; name the surface appearance that hides it.
5. likelihood: a probability with a basis ("evidence" | "base-rate" |
   "judgment") and a rationale. No naked numbers.
6. indicators: at least 2 observable early warnings, each with WHERE to watch
   (a named tender portal, an operator's capex call, a standards docket, a
   vendor's price list) and the reading that should trigger re-assessment.
7. mitigations: at least 2. Where the right move is to buy information, use
   type "information" and name the ledger node it de-risks (voiNodeId) — its
   value will be computed, not asserted.

QUOTAS (per lens): at least one hypothesis self-tagged expectedObviousness
"low", and at least one whose perturbation targets NONE of tamBase /
serviceableFactor / obtainableFactor — the tornado already covers those bands;
look past them.

MINE THE RESIDUALS: the highest-yield material is where sources disagree with
the curated value (the digests' "tensions"), where searches came back empty
(the digests' "absences" — an unquantified market is a risk, not a comfort),
where a derivation's crossCheck diverges from its primary method, and where a
precise-looking value carries maturity "needs-source".

HOW YOUR CLAIM BECOMES A NUMBER (the perturbation contract):
- You never state € impacts. You emit machine-readable ops; the engine computes
  ΔTAM/ΔSAM/ΔYAM by re-running the real funnel. Any € figure you write in prose
  will be stripped.
- Ops: {nodeId, op: "set"|"scale"|"add", value} rewrite one leaf's value;
  {nodeId, op: "exclude"} removes a dimension cell from the venture's reachable
  scope ("unavailable to us" — foreclosure), which is different from shrinking it.
- Ops apply in array order. Ratio nodes must stay inside [0,1]; tamBase must stay > 0.
- Only FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes may be perturbed (marked in the ledger you receive).
  shape.cagr and shape.cr3 are context — a demand-pace or concentration risk must
  be translated into the funnel nodes it would actually move (tamBase level,
  serviceableFactor reach, obtainableFactor pace, or a share).
- Sibling shares are NOT re-normalized when you move one share (documented v1
  simplification). If you mean a mix-SHIFT, perturb multiple siblings; if you
  mean "this slice is smaller in absolute terms", one op is correct.
- Perturbing an assumption leaf overrides whatever the user set: your claim is
  about the world, not about their slider.

BANNED GENRES (emitting one of these wastes your slot):
- "Find 2 customers to talk to and get an LOI" — an action item, not a risk. It names no node, no mechanism, no falsifier.
- "Do research on customer buying behaviour" — a homework assignment any consultant could write for any company.
- "Competition is strong / the market is concentrated" — the ledger already states CR3. Restating a fact is not a risk; the risk is a MECHANISM that concentration enables.
- "The market may be smaller than expected" — the tamBase band (240–520) already says this. Only worth saying if you name WHICH derivation hop fails and WHY.
- "Regulatory changes could impact the business" — name the instrument, the compliance artifact it requires, and the date it lands, or drop it.
- "The band's low end might be true" with no new reason — the band is already in the model; you are paid for what is NOT in the model.

GOLD EXEMPLAR (the target register — do NOT repeat its content):
{
 "id": "busway-hollowing-hyperscale",
 "title": "Busway + OCP power shelves hollow out the hyperscale 44% from inside the scope boundary",
 "narrative": "The model excludes busbars and power shelves as out-of-scope substitutes — a scoping footnote. But hyperscale is 44% of TAM, and it is precisely the segment whose new AI-density builds are moving power distribution from rack PDUs to overhead busway feeding OCP-style in-rack power shelves. If that architecture becomes the default for new CE hyperscale capacity, the largest slice of this TAM is not 'ours to win slowly' — it is structurally shrinking while the headline number stays flat. The exclusion list quietly became the market thesis.",
 "category": "boundary",
 "targetNodes": [
  "seg.hyperscale"
 ],
 "mechanism": [
  {
   "step": "trigger",
   "text": "New hyperscale AI-density builds adopt busway + in-rack DC power shelves instead of discrete rack PDUs",
   "refs": [
    "digest:seg-mix#c?",
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "CE hyperscale capacity additions (the growth the 9% CAGR is made of) carry falling rack-PDU content per MW; replacement demand in legacy halls does not offset it",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:shape.cagr",
    "digest:tam-base#c?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "model-impact",
   "text": "The hyperscale share of the rack-PDU market shrinks in absolute terms — seg.hyperscale 0.44 overstates the addressable slice for a new entrant's window",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  }
 ],
 "whyMissable": "Substitutes were explicitly considered and excluded — the boundary decision looks handled. The kill path is not 'we misjudged the boundary' but 'the boundary moved into the biggest segment while the TAM base year stayed put'.",
 "expectedObviousness": "low",
 "falsifier": "2025–26 CE hyperscale fit-out specs (or Schneider/Vertiv rack-power order mixes) showing intelligent rack PDUs holding share in new AI-density halls.",
 "likelihood": {
  "value": 0.35,
  "rationale": "OCP power-shelf adoption is documented for AI racks, but CE colocation and enterprise retrofits (56% of TAM) keep buying rack PDUs; timing within a 12-month window is uncertain.",
  "basis": "evidence"
 },
 "perturbation": [
  {
   "nodeId": "seg.hyperscale",
   "op": "set",
   "value": 0.22,
   "note": "≈half of the hyperscale slice migrates to busway/shelf architectures"
  }
 ],
 "indicators": [
  {
   "signal": "Rack-power architecture specified in new CE hyperscale tenders (busway/shelf vs PDU)",
   "where": "Frankfurt/Amsterdam data-center fit-out tenders; OCP regional summit deployment talks",
   "threshold": "≥2 flagship CE builds specifying shelf-based distribution",
   "updates": "increases"
  },
  {
   "signal": "Incumbent rack-PDU revenue mix commentary (Vertiv/Schneider earnings)",
   "where": "Quarterly earnings calls, rack-power product-line commentary",
   "threshold": "PDU line flat while busway line grows double-digit",
   "updates": "increases"
  }
 ],
 "mitigations": [
  {
   "action": "Commission a CE rack-power architecture survey across the 2026 hyperscale build pipeline",
   "type": "information",
   "voiNodeId": "seg.hyperscale"
  },
  {
   "action": "Qualify the product line for colocation/enterprise retrofit first, where PDU form-factor is sticky",
   "type": "strategic"
  }
 ]
}

content: {"hypotheses":[{"id":"enterprise-gpu-busbar-half-refuted","title":"The 'refuted' GPU-pod doubt was only half-refuted: dense enterprise AI refresh ships on DC busbar, hollowing seg.enterprise from both ends","narrative":"Refinement cycle 1 closed the 'GPU pods skip PDUs' doubt because NVIDIA reference architectures ship PDU variants. But the vendor publishes parallel architectures: the AC/PDU version is the fallback, while the flagship dense design routes power via DC busbar with materially lower PDU attach per rack. The only part of enterprise on-premise that is growing is exactly this GPU-pod build-out; the legacy base is bleeding ~2pp of capacity share per year toward 19% by 2031. So the 0.29 enterprise slice is squeezed twice — the shrinking legacy half keeps buying PDUs it already owns, and the growing AI half is steered toward the substitute. A checkbox marked 'confirmed' is carrying 29% of the TAM.","category":"boundary","targetNodes":["seg.enterprise"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"Enterprise on-premise capacity growth is now driven almost entirely by GenAI/GPU infrastructure after a sustained no-growth period, and the flagship reference architecture for the densest pods uses DC busbar with sharply reduced rack-PDU attach, with the AC/PDU variant positioned as legacy fallback","refs":["digest:seg-mix#c19","ledger:seg.enterprise"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The growing sub-segment of enterprise carries falling PDU content per rack while the legacy on-prem base shrinks ~2pp/year (from 32% end-2025 toward 19% by 2031) — effective enterprise rack-PDU demand falls on both the growth side and the base side simultaneously","refs":["digest:seg-mix#c5","digest:seg-mix#c4"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"seg.enterprise 0.29 (crossCheck marked 'value confirmed') overstates the PDU-addressable enterprise slice; during the venture's 12-month window the realistic value sits below the model's own 0.24 low band, and the funnel's second-largest segment quietly deflates","refs":["ledger:seg.enterprise","digest:seg-mix#c2"]}],"whyMissable":"The doubt was explicitly investigated and stamped 'refuted' in refinement cycle 1 — the risk register shows a closed item. The binary confirmed/refuted framing hides that PDU-shipping and PDU-attach-per-MW are different questions: NVIDIA shipping PDU variants does not mean the dense builds that constitute all the segment's growth actually use them.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"Attach-rate evidence from 2026 CE enterprise GPU-pod deployments (integrator BOMs or vendor order data) showing rack-PDU counts per rack at or above the legacy ≥3-per-rack norm in the majority of dense installs.","likelihood":{"value":0.4,"rationale":"The parallel-architecture steering is documented in vendor reference designs and the enterprise share decline (2pp/year) is a multi-vintage Synergy trend; uncertainty is whether CE enterprises actually deploy the flagship density tier within 12 months or stay on air-cooled AC/PDU designs","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"seg.enterprise","op":"set","value":0.2,"note":"GPU-driven growth adopts busbar attach while legacy base decays; effective PDU-addressable enterprise share drops below the model's 0.24 low band"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Which power variant (AC/PDU vs DC busbar) is default in NVIDIA/OEM pod reference-architecture documentation revisions","where":"NVIDIA GTC reference-architecture sessions and DGX/MGX pod deployment guides; Dell/HPE AI-factory configurators","threshold":"DC busbar becomes the documented default for ≥50% of published dense-pod configurations","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Enterprise/IT-segment rack-PDU revenue commentary from incumbents","where":"Vertiv and Schneider quarterly earnings calls, rack-power and IT-channel product-line commentary","threshold":"Enterprise PDU line flat or declining while integrated-rack/busbar lines grow double-digit for two consecutive quarters","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Commission an attach-rate teardown of CE enterprise GPU-pod installs (PDUs per rack by density tier) via 5–8 integrator interviews","type":"information","voiNodeId":"seg.enterprise","note":"Directly re-bases the enterprise slice on measured attach rather than a binary confirmed/refuted flag"},{"action":"Position the Year-1 product for the air-cooled enterprise refresh tier (where AC/PDU remains default) and roadmap a high-amp/DC-compatible SKU for dense pods","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Decouples Year-1 revenue from the sub-segment where substitution bites first"}]},{"id":"colo-spec-contagion-from-hyperscale-tenants","title":"Wholesale colocation inherits hyperscaler power specs: the 18% colo slice — modeled with zero sensitivity band — follows the substitute out of category","narrative":"The model treats colocation as the PDU-native safe harbor: seg.colocation carries a null sensitivity band, so it never appears in the tornado at all. But CBRE shows European colo take-up being outstripped by hyperscaler and AI/HPC tenant demand — and in wholesale/prelet halls the tenant, not the operator, dictates the rack power architecture. If hyperscale tenants specify busway plus power shelves inside leased halls (the same migration the exemplar tracks in self-build), the colo segment's PDU content leaves the category through the tenant spec sheet while the operator's capacity numbers keep growing 19% a year. The one segment the engine treats as certain is the one with the untracked contagion path.","category":"boundary","targetNodes":["seg.colocation"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"European colocation take-up is expected to outstrip new supply driven specifically by hyperscaler and AI/HPC tenants — the demand filling CE colo halls is the same buyer cohort migrating to busway/power-shelf architectures, and in wholesale preleases that tenant writes the fit-out power spec","refs":["digest:seg-mix#c15","digest:seg-mix#c13"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"As hyperscale tenants standardize substitute architectures inside leased halls, colo PDU content per MW falls even as colo capacity grows near double-digit and supply across the top-5 markets grows a record 20% — capacity metrics stay healthy while category-specific demand erodes","refs":["digest:seg-mix#c18","digest:seg-mix#c16"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"seg.colocation 0.18 is single-source with a null band: the engine registers zero downside for this slice, so a tenant-spec-driven erosion of colo PDU demand is structurally invisible in every sensitivity output the client's team has reviewed","refs":["ledger:seg.colocation","digest:seg-mix#c1"]}],"whyMissable":"Colocation reads as the segment where rack PDUs are structurally mandatory (per-rack metering, multi-tenant billing), and the node's null band means it never surfaces in any sensitivity view — the team's eyes are drawn to banded nodes. The kill path runs through the wholesale/retail split inside the 18%, a distinction the model does not carry at all.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"Fit-out specifications from 2026 CE wholesale colocation preleases (Frankfurt, Amsterdam) showing rack PDUs — tenant-supplied or operator-supplied — as the standard rack power distribution in AI-density halls rather than busway/shelf architectures.","likelihood":{"value":0.3,"rationale":"Tenant spec authority in wholesale preleases is well documented and the buyer cohort is verifiably the migrating one, but retail colo and standard-density wholesale (the majority of current CE colo MW) remain PDU-native; the risk concentrates in the AI-density prelet growth tranche within the 12-month window","basis":"judgment"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"seg.colocation","op":"set","value":0.12,"note":"Wholesale/prelet AI-density share of colo (roughly a third of the slice) migrates to tenant-specified busway/shelf architectures"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Rack power architecture named in wholesale prelease fit-out specifications for AI-density halls","where":"Frankfurt and Amsterdam wholesale colo prelease announcements and fit-out tender documents; DCD/DCNN build coverage","threshold":"≥2 CE wholesale preleases specifying busway/power-shelf distribution as tenant standard","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Colo operator capex-call language on standardizing power distribution for AI halls","where":"Digital Realty and Equinix EMEA quarterly capex calls and investor-day fit-out cost breakdowns","threshold":"Explicit mention of busway or shelf standardization for new AI capacity","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Commission a CE colo segmentation study splitting the 18% slice into retail vs wholesale/prelet MW and the power architecture specified in each","type":"information","voiNodeId":"seg.colocation","note":"Replaces a null-band single-source node with a composition-aware estimate — the tornado currently cannot see this node at all"},{"action":"Anchor Year-1 sales in retail and managed colocation, where per-outlet metered PDUs are tied to tenant billing and remain architecturally sticky","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Retail colo billing requirements make PDU displacement commercially costly for the operator"}]},{"id":"ce7-hyperscale-mix-mirage","title":"The 0.91 rescale imports hyperscale that isn't in CE-7: the segment mix is misallocated even though the TAM headline doesn't move","narrative":"seg.hyperscale applies Synergy's global 48% hyperscale capacity share to Central Europe via a flat 0.91 carve-out. But ~60% of Europe's operational hyperscale self-build capacity sits in Ireland, the Netherlands, Sweden and Belgium — three of the four outside CE-7 — and Europe's self-build growth (24% YoY) keeps landing disproportionately in those markets. CE-7 is structurally colo- and enterprise-weighted relative to the global mix the model borrowed. Because segment shares sum to the same base, the TAM headline is untouched and no alarm rings; but the SAM haircut logic, certification priorities and Year-1 pipeline are all built on a buyer mix that overweights hyperscale by perhaps a third. This is a resourcing kill, not a sizing kill.","category":"model-structure","targetNodes":["seg.hyperscale","seg.colocation","seg.enterprise"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"Synergy's global 48% hyperscale share is transplanted to CE-7 through a flat 0.91 carve-out, but Europe's hyperscale self-build capacity is concentrated (~60%) in Ireland, NL, Sweden and Belgium — a geographic pattern that makes the global split a poor proxy for CE-7","refs":["digest:seg-mix#c0","digest:seg-mix#c14"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"European hyperscale self-build grows 24% YoY versus colo's 19%, and that growth keeps concentrating outside CE-7, so the divergence between the borrowed global mix and the true CE-7 mix widens through exactly the venture's entry window rather than converging","refs":["digest:seg-mix#c12","digest:seg-mix#c13"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The €300M base is misallocated across segments: hyperscale overweighted, colo/enterprise underweighted. Since shares sum to the same TAM, the headline never flags the error — but which buyers to certify for, which channels to build and where Year-1 pipeline effort goes are all keyed to the wrong weights","refs":["ledger:seg.hyperscale","ledger:seg.colocation","ledger:seg.enterprise"]}],"whyMissable":"A wrong segment mix leaves TAM, SAM and YAM totals identical, so every number the client's team has reviewed reads the same; the tornado tests each share's band in isolation but never tests whether the mix as a whole was transplanted from the wrong geography. The 0.91 carve-out looks like a careful adjustment, which disguises that the underlying split was never Europe-scoped.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"A Europe- or CE-scoped capacity split (CBRE, Synergy regional cut, or JLL) showing hyperscale-owned/operated capacity at ≥40% of CE-7 MW, validating the transplanted global share.","likelihood":{"value":0.45,"rationale":"The 60%-outside-CE concentration figure is directly sourced and the ledger's own promotion path admits no Europe-scoped split exists; the main uncertainty is how much hyperscale leased-in-colo capacity within CE offsets the self-build concentration elsewhere","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"seg.hyperscale","op":"set","value":0.3,"note":"CE-7 hyperscale-owned share corrected for self-build concentration outside CE"},{"nodeId":"seg.colocation","op":"set","value":0.26,"note":"Mix-shift: CE capacity is more colo-weighted than the global split implies"},{"nodeId":"seg.enterprise","op":"set","value":0.33,"note":"Mix-shift: DACH enterprise/self-operated weight (BMWK, Swiss self-operation) raises the enterprise share; siblings moved explicitly since the engine does not re-normalize"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"First publication of a Europe or CE regional capacity split by operator type","where":"Synergy Research regional releases; CBRE European Data Centres quarterly reports","threshold":"CE/Europe hyperscale-owned share reported ≥8pp below the model's 0.44","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Location of announced hyperscale self-build campuses in Europe","where":"DCD/DCNN build announcements; German and Polish planning/permit registries for hyperscale campuses","threshold":"≥70% of announced European self-build MW landing outside CE-7 for two consecutive quarters","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Purchase or commission a CE-7-scoped capacity split by operator type (hyperscale-owned vs colo vs enterprise) before locking Year-1 channel investment","type":"information","voiNodeId":"seg.hyperscale","note":"Replaces the transplanted global split — the single highest-leverage structural fix in the segment dimension"},{"action":"Weight Year-1 pipeline and certification spend toward colo and enterprise accounts in DE/NL rather than hyperscale framework pursuit","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Hedges the mix error: these buyers exist in CE at scale under either mix scenario"}]},{"id":"intelligent-subset-scope-mismatch","title":"The venture sells intelligent PDUs; the TAM counts all rack PDUs — the category boundary mismatch is inside the model, not at its edge","narrative":"Every boundary discussion has focused on excluded substitutes (busway, shelves). The unexamined boundary is in the other direction: the product is intelligent rack PDUs, but tamBase is explicitly scoped as the 'Central Europe rack-PDU market, all segments/buyers' — including basic and metered-only units the venture does not sell. IndexBox itself maintains a separate EU 'Intelligent Rack PDUs' report distinct from its 'Rack PDUs' report, i.e., the industry's own taxonomy treats these as different products. No retrieved source states the intelligent share of European rack-PDU revenue, and the 10–12% intelligent growth figure the model cites traces to a far broader power-and-cable-management report. The ±40% band prices measurement error on the whole category; it says nothing about what fraction of that category the venture can even quote into. Both errors stack.","category":"boundary","targetNodes":["tamBase"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"The product is intelligent rack PDUs while tamBase covers all rack PDUs; IndexBox sells 'Intelligent Rack PDUs' and 'Rack PDUs' as separate EU reports, confirming the vendor taxonomy splits the subset the venture sells from the category the model sized","refs":["profile:Product","ledger:tamBase","digest:tam-base#c14"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The intelligent subset's cited 10–12% growth comes from the €8.5–9.5B EU Power-and-Cable-Management report — a category far broader than rack PDUs — and the directly-scoped rack-panel figure grows only 5–8%; no source quantifies the intelligent share of EU rack-PDU revenue, so the subset fraction is unknown and plausibly well below 1.0","refs":["digest:tam-base#c4","digest:tam-base#c5"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"The €300M base overstates the venture's category-true TAM by whatever revenue share basic/metered-only PDUs hold in CE; the sensitivity band (240–360) models measurement error of the full category, orthogonal to and stackable with the subset mismatch — and the one report that would resolve it sits behind a $4,000 paywall","refs":["ledger:tamBase","digest:tam-base#c12"]}],"whyMissable":"'Rack PDU' reads as one category and intelligent PDUs are its premium tier, so value share is assumed to approximate category revenue; the wide ±40% band creates the comfortable impression that any downside is already priced in. The band and the subset mismatch are independent errors, and the founder's risk list treats the band as covering both.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"IndexBox EU Intelligent Rack PDUs headline (or the Omdia rack-PDU tracker's segmentation) showing intelligent/managed units at ≥80% of EU rack-PDU revenue, confirming the subset ≈ category in value terms.","likelihood":{"value":0.4,"rationale":"Data-center buyers skew heavily toward metered/switched units and intelligent SKUs carry a large price premium, which pulls value share up — but basic PDUs still dominate unit volumes in enterprise and retail-colo racks, and no source pins the revenue split; genuine coin-flip territory on whether the mismatch exceeds ~25% of the base","basis":"judgment"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"tamBase","op":"scale","value":0.65,"note":"Intelligent (monitored/switched) PDUs assumed to hold ~65% of CE rack-PDU revenue; the remainder is basic/metered volume outside the venture's product boundary"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Published intelligent-vs-total rack-PDU revenue split for EU or Germany","where":"IndexBox EU/Germany Intelligent Rack PDUs report headlines; Omdia Rack PDU Annual Market Analysis abstract updates","threshold":"Intelligent share reported below 70% of category revenue","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"SKU mix stocked and quoted by CE distributors (basic vs metered vs switched/intelligent)","where":"CE electrical/IT distributor price lists and catalogs (e.g., Rexel, Also/Ingram DC-infrastructure lines)","threshold":"Basic+metered-only SKUs constituting the majority of listed rack-PDU line items","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Commission the IndexBox EU Intelligent Rack PDUs report (and Germany country cut) to pin the subset share directly","type":"information","voiNodeId":"tamBase","note":"~$4–8K resolves both the subset fraction and narrows the category band — the ledger's own promotion path, extended to the correct sub-report"},{"action":"Add a low-cost metered-only SKU tier to the launch portfolio so quotes can cover the full category while the intelligent tier carries margin","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Converts the boundary mismatch from foreclosed demand into an entry wedge"}]},{"id":"nl-selfbuild-hollows-second-geography","title":"The best-triangulated geography is the trap: NL's 28% sits on a capped colo base while its growth migrates to self-build architectures a regional entrant can't quote into","narrative":"geo.NL is the model's most reassuring node — Statista and the Dutch Data Center Association agree within 3%. But that triangulation validates yesterday's colocation stock, not the composition of forward demand. The Netherlands is one of four countries holding ~60% of Europe's hyperscale self-build capacity, and self-build is growing 24% YoY versus colo's 19% — while Amsterdam (≈70% of NL colo) is supply-constrained. So the venture's second-largest geography weight (28% of the base) rests on a slice where the PDU-native part is capped and the growing part procures through hyperscaler global frameworks and busway/shelf architectures that a pre-launch regional entrant cannot reach in its first 12 months. The MW totals will keep rising; the biddable NL demand won't.","category":"boundary","targetNodes":["geo.NL"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"NL is among the four countries concentrating ~60% of Europe's operational hyperscale self-build capacity, and European self-build additions grow 24% YoY — in NL, forward capacity growth increasingly happens outside the 951MW colo base that geo.NL's 0.28 apportionment measures","refs":["digest:seg-mix#c14","digest:seg-mix#c12","ledger:geo.NL"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Self-build capacity procures power distribution through hyperscaler global specs rather than regional channels, while the colo segment — larger but slower at 19% YoY and slowly losing share of total capacity — is the only NL demand a new CE entrant can actually bid; Amsterdam supply constraints cap even that","refs":["digest:seg-mix#c13","digest:seg-mix#c18"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"geo.NL 0.28 overstates the entrant-reachable Dutch slice during the pre-launch first-12-months window: the ratio is anchored to a stalling colo stock while the growth it implicitly promises accrues to out-of-category, out-of-channel self-build","refs":["ledger:geo.NL","profile:Stage"]}],"whyMissable":"geo.NL carries the ledger's cleanest triangulation (two independent sources within 3%), so it reads as the one geography nobody needs to re-examine. Agreement between sources measuring the same colo stock says nothing about whether that stock is the demand a Year-1 entrant will face — the precision of the snapshot hides the composition shift.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"2026 NL capacity-addition evidence (DDCA annual report plus fit-out tenders) showing new Dutch MW — including hyperscale campuses — specifying rack PDUs procured through channels open to regional vendors at rates proportional to NL's 28% weight.","likelihood":{"value":0.35,"rationale":"The self-build concentration and growth differential are directly sourced and Amsterdam's constraints are well documented; the offset is that NL retail/wholesale colo refresh demand is real and PDU-native, so the question is degree — whether reachable NL demand supports 0.28 or something closer to 0.20–0.22 of the base","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"geo.NL","op":"set","value":0.22,"note":"NL weight corrected to the colo-anchored, entrant-reachable slice; self-build growth and capped Amsterdam supply removed from the effective ratio"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"NL colocation capacity growth vs hyperscale self-build additions","where":"Dutch Data Center Association 'State of the Dutch Data Centers' annual report; CBRE Amsterdam market quarterlies","threshold":"NL colo net additions <5% YoY while national self-build MW grows >20%","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Grid-connection allocations for new NL data-center projects","where":"Liander/TenneT congestion and connection-queue publications for the Amsterdam metro and Middenmeer/Eemshaven zones","threshold":"New allocations flowing predominantly to hyperscale campus projects rather than colo expansions","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Build a bottom-up NL reachable-demand model: named colo operators × refresh cycle × racks, replacing the top-down capacity ratio for the second-largest geography","type":"information","voiNodeId":"geo.NL","note":"Converts a stock-based apportionment into a biddable-demand figure before NL channel investment is committed"},{"action":"Sequence market entry DE-first and treat NL as an Amsterdam retail-colo refresh play rather than a proportional 28% of pipeline effort","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Germany's larger, enterprise-heavy base is less exposed to the self-build migration in the Year-1 window"}]}]}
